fuzzy_prd:
We need a small shared utility library for programs that manage workloads on individual cluster nodes, addressing two recurring pain points.

First, a standardized way to tag errors with a semantic category so calling code can ask 'did this fail because the thing was not there?' or 'was the input invalid?' without fragile string matching. The tagging must be nil-safe — attaching a category to a missing error should produce nothing. Category checks must work even when the tagged error has been wrapped by other errors up the call stack, provided those wrappers expose their underlying cause. Third-party error types that self-report category membership via a boolean method should also be recognized.

Second, a simple in-memory lookup layer for the four core object types that node agents need: compute units, networking endpoints, credential stores, and configuration bundles. A caller pre-fills the store with current node objects, then either counts all records of a type or fetches one by its two-part identifier. Fetch failures on data-bearing types should handle it the same way as the error categorization module does for not-found conditions, and the failure message must name the object type and the missing identifier.

Data from a successful fetch must be returned with entries in a consistent, deterministic order so results are reproducible across environments.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func NewResourceManager(podLister corev1listers.PodLister, secretLister corev1listers.SecretLister, configMapLister corev1listers.ConfigMapLister, serviceLister corev1listers.ServiceLister) (*ResourceManager, error)

trigger_keywords: what happens when AsNotFound receives nil
tagging a nil error with not-found category
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: AsNotFound(nil) returns nil — not a categorized error, not a member of any category. IsNotFound(AsNotFound(nil)) == false. This nil-safety applies equally to AsInvalidInput(nil).

trigger_keywords: how is error membership checked through wrapped errors
causal chain traversal for error categories
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: IsNotFound and IsInvalidInput walk the causal chain by checking for a 'Cause() error' interface (as implemented by github.com/pkg/errors). If the outer error implements the relevant interface, it is checked directly; otherwise the chain is followed recursively via Cause(). A notFoundError wrapped with errors.Wrap (which exposes Cause()) is still recognized as a member.

trigger_keywords: what message format for missing secret
not found error message format for resources
how is the not-found message rendered for configmap
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The not-found message for a missing resource uses the format: <kind> "<name>" not found — for example: secret "name-X" not found or configmap "name-X" not found. The kind string matches the resource type used in the request (secret or configmap, lowercase).

trigger_keywords: how is data rendered for secret or configmap fetch
output format for fetched resource data
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Data from a successful secret or configmap fetch is printed as: data=<comma-joined key=value pairs with keys in ascending sorted (lexicographic) order>. For example, if data has key-0=val-0 and key-1=val-1, output is: data=key-0=val-0,key-1=val-1

trigger_keywords: what does a foreign error with self-reporting boolean output as its message
custom error boolean flag message text
third-party error message when flag is false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A foreign error that implements the category interface via a boolean flag has its Error() method return the lowercase string representation of the boolean: 'true' when the flag is true, 'false' when the flag is false. So IsNotFound on customCategorizedError(false) returns false and its message is 'false'; on customCategorizedError(true) returns true with message 'true'.

trigger_keywords: what is the classify output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The classify action always prints exactly three lines in this order: 'category=<category_name>
', 'classified=<true|false>
', 'message=<error_message_or_empty>
'. The message line is present even when there is no error — in that case it is 'message=
' (empty value).

trigger_keywords: how is a categorized error wrapped in cause
what error wrapper is used when wrap_in_cause is true
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When wrap_in_cause is true, the categorized error is wrapped using errors.Wrap(err, "some details") from github.com/pkg/errors. The wrapped error's message becomes 'some details: <original message>' and it still tests as a member of the original category because IsNotFound/IsInvalidInput traverses the Cause() chain.

trigger_keywords: what parameters does the resource manager constructor take
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: NewResourceManager takes four parameters in this order: (1) podLister corev1listers.PodLister, (2) secretLister corev1listers.SecretLister, (3) configMapLister corev1listers.ConfigMapLister, (4) serviceLister corev1listers.ServiceLister. It returns (*ResourceManager, error). Any of these listers may be nil if that resource type is not needed.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way as the error categorization module does for not-found conditions
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Resource fetch failures (GetSecret, GetConfigMap) should return errors recognized by IsNotFound() — i.e., errors produced via errdefs.NotFound() or errdefs.AsNotFound() — and the dispatcher prints 'error=not_found\nmessage=<kind> "<name>" not found\n' mirroring the classify output's error=not_found sentinel.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question doesn't match a documented constraint. Please refer to the golden source files in errdefs/ and manager/ for authoritative behavior, or ask about a specific feature such as error category membership, nil-safety, causal chain traversal, data sort order, or resource manager constructor parameters.

feature1_1_not_found.json.description: An error value is constructed in one of several ways and then queried for membership in the not-found category. Construction variants are: building a categorized error directly from a message; tagging an existing plain error with the category, which is a nil-safe operation that yields no error when there is no underlying error; supplying no error at all; or providing a foreign error that advertises its own category membership through a boolean self-report. The query walks the chain of wrapped causes, so a categorized error stays recognizable even after being wrapped by an unrelated cause-bearing error. The output reports the queried category name, whether the value is recognized as a member of that category, and the value's textual message (empty when there is no error).

feature1_2_invalid_input.json.description: An error value is constructed in one of several ways and then queried for membership in the invalid-input category. Construction variants are: building a categorized error directly from a message; tagging an existing plain error with the category, which is a nil-safe operation that yields no error when there is no underlying error; supplying no error at all; or providing a foreign error that advertises its own category membership through a boolean self-report. The query walks the chain of wrapped causes, so a categorized error stays recognizable even after being wrapped by an unrelated cause-bearing error. The output reports the queried category name, whether the value is recognized as a member of that category, and the value's textual message (empty when there is no error).

feature2_1_pods.json.description: A set of pod records is loaded into the resource cache, each identified by a namespace and a name. The cache acts as a passthrough to the underlying store, and listing all pods returns every record that was loaded. The output reports the number of pods the cache returns.

feature2_2_services.json.description: A set of service records is loaded into the resource cache, each identified by a namespace and a name. The cache acts as a passthrough to the underlying store, and listing all services returns every record that was loaded. The output reports the number of services the cache returns.

feature2_3_secret.json.description: Secret records are loaded into the resource cache, each identified by a namespace and a name and carrying a map of key/value data. A retrieval is then performed by name and namespace. When a matching record exists, its data is returned and the output lists the key/value pairs (keys sorted). When no record matches the requested name and namespace, the retrieval fails with a not-found outcome whose message names the requested resource kind and name.

feature2_4_configmap.json.description: Config-map records are loaded into the resource cache, each identified by a namespace and a name and carrying a map of key/value data. A retrieval is then performed by name and namespace. When a matching record exists, its data is returned and the output lists the key/value pairs (keys sorted). When no record matches the requested name and namespace, the retrieval fails with a not-found outcome whose message names the requested resource kind and name.
